mnemonics.title: CMMI Maturity Levels
mnemonics.content: IPROD: Initial → Repeatable (Managed) → Defined → Quantitatively Managed → Optimizing

mnemonics.title: PDCA Cycle
mnemonics.content: P DCA: Plan → Do → Check → Act (Continuous Improvement Loop)

mnemonics.title: Six Sigma DMAIC
mnemonics.content: D MDAIC: Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control

mnemonics.title: ISO/IEC 25010 Product Characteristics
mnemonics.content: FPSU-RSP: Functional Suitability, Performance Efficiency, Security, Usability, Reliability, Maintainability, Portability

acronyms.usage: Framework for process improvement with 5 maturity levels
Organization-wide quality approach focusing on customer satisfaction
Using statistics to monitor and control processes
Product reliability metric - average time between system failures
Six Sigma measure of defect rate
Process-focused quality activities to prevent defects
Framework for managing quality in organizations

visualTips.visualization: Think of a pyramid with 5 levels: Level 1 (base) = ad-hoc processes, Level 5 (top) = optimizing processes. Each level builds on the previous one.
Use a factory analogy: Process = how the factory machines run (methods, procedures), Product = what comes off the assembly line (the software). Good machines (process) produce good products.
A circular flow diagram: Plan (design the change) → Do (implement it) → Check (measure results) → Act (standardize or restart). It keeps循环 (looping) for continuous improvement.
An iceberg model - Prevention and Appraisal are above water (visible), Failure costs are below water (hidden but larger). The goal is to invest in prevention to reduce failure costs.
Think of 6 sigma as 99.99966% perfect - if you produce 1 million items, only about 3.4 will be defective. That's nearly perfect!

analogies.analogy: Like comparing a finished painting (product) to the artist's technique and tools (process). A skilled artist with good tools creates better paintings.
Like learning to drive: Level 1 = nervous learner with unpredictable driving; Level 5 = experienced driver who automatically follows safe practices and continuously improves.
QA is like preventive medicine (keeping you healthy), while QC is like diagnostic tests (finding problems after they exist).
Like climbing a spiral staircase - you keep going around but always moving upward. Each rotation (cycle) makes you slightly better.
Like car maintenance - prevention (regular oil changes) costs less than failure (engine breakdown). Investing in quality saves money long-term.
